verses.text: Eliphaz speaks first, recalling how Job once strengthened the weak and steadied the stumbling. He begins by honouring Job's past. There is courtesy in his opening, yet a sting is being prepared beneath the praise.

Eliphaz speaks first, recalling how Job once strengthened the weak and steadied the stumbling. He begins by honouring Job's past. There is courtesy in his opening, yet a sting is being prepared beneath the praise.

But now, he says, trouble has come and Job faints; is not his reverence for God his confidence and hope? He hints that real faith would not so collapse. He misjudges grief, mistaking honest anguish for failed devotion.

But now, he says, trouble has come and Job faints; is not his reverence for God his confidence and hope? He hints that real faith would not so collapse. He misjudges grief, mistaking honest anguish for failed devotion.

But now, he says, trouble has come and Job faints; is not his reverence for God his confidence and hope? He hints that real faith would not so collapse. He misjudges grief, mistaking honest anguish for failed devotion.

'Who ever perished, being innocent?' Eliphaz asks; those who plow iniquity reap it. He lays down the rigid rule that suffering proves sin. The principle holds often, but as a universal law it is false, and it wounds the very man it should comfort.

'Who ever perished, being innocent?' Eliphaz asks; those who plow iniquity reap it. He lays down the rigid rule that suffering proves sin. The principle holds often, but as a universal law it is false, and it wounds the very man it should comfort.

He pictures the lions, fierce and strong, perishing for lack of prey — the wicked brought to nothing. His point is that evil destroys itself. True in general, but cruelly misapplied to a righteous sufferer.

He pictures the lions, fierce and strong, perishing for lack of prey — the wicked brought to nothing. His point is that evil destroys itself. True in general, but cruelly misapplied to a righteous sufferer.

Eliphaz tells of a night vision, a spirit passing, hair standing up, silence, then a voice. He clothes his argument in awe. Yet a dramatic experience is no proof of truth; even solemn impressions must be weighed by God's word.

Eliphaz tells of a night vision, a spirit passing, hair standing up, silence, then a voice. He clothes his argument in awe. Yet a dramatic experience is no proof of truth; even solemn impressions must be weighed by God's word.

The voice asked, 'shall mortal man be more just than God?' — if He charges even angels with folly, how much more frail man, crushed like a moth. The truth that none is pure before God is real. But Eliphaz wields it to silence Job rather than to lift him.
